pages_preview.text_preview: Handout of the Replication Study “Why didn’t you tag me?!: Social exclusion from Instagram posts hurts, especially those with a high need to belong” S23 - 1 many lab replication –Theresa Brückner, Anna Frey, Minja Bojic, Yan Benjamin Kohse 1. Aim and Motivation of the Study The aim of this study is to replicate Study 1 of the existing scientific study ‘’Why didn’t you tag me?! Social exclusion from Instagram posts hurts, especially those with a high need to belong.’’ by Christiane M. Büttner and Selma C. Rudert (2022) and deepen the understanding of social media ostracism and its effects on mood and need satisfaction. We aimed to achieve this by adding another social media platform (Facebook) to our research and by collecting participants from different cultural backgrounds. 2. Social Benefits of our Study The widespread adoption of social media platforms is prevalent and in 2022, 4.59 billion people worldwide were using social media (Number of Worldwide Social Network Users 2027, o. J.) Thereof, exploration of situations on social media that may cause negative feelings and perception of exclusion represents a huge social benefit of our and the original study. Based on our state of research, Büttner and Rudert (2022) were the first to include not being tagged as a form of social ostracism. They showed that exclusion situations significantly affected need satisfaction scores within their sample (N = 176). In consequence, we were interested to see if we would find similar effects. In case that ‘not being tagged’ represents a threat to the feeling of inclusion in the general population, more awareness for these effects should be spread and specific processes behind explored. In order to extend the scope of social ostracism on social media compared to Büttner and Rudert (2022), we chose to include individuals that use Facebook more 1

frequently than Instagram. We perceived Facebook to be a good choice as it was the most used platform in Germany in 2023 (Social Networks nach Nutzern 2023, o. J.). 3. State of Research The term "ostracism," as defined by the Cambridge Dictionary, refers to the intentional exclusion of a person from a social group or activity (Cambridge University Press & Assessment, 2024). This study focuses on social media ostracism, specifically ostracism occurring on social media platforms. A brief overview of existing research on social media ostracism reveals several findings. Numerous studies support the idea that social media ostracism may elicit negative emotions, such as sadness (Schneider et al., 2017), and threaten fundamental needs, including those for belonging, self-esteem, control, and meaningful existence (Lutz & Schneider, 2021). It's important to note that social ostracism on social media can manifest in various forms, such as not being tagged in posts or stories on Instagram, exclusion from posted photos (Büttner & Rudert, 2022), and lack of responses to status updates on Facebook (Lutz & Schneider, 2021; Tobin et al., 2015). Büttner and Rudert's study (2022) explored the "need to belong" as a possible moderator of need threat following social media ostracism, revealing a significant main effect. Sarita and Suleeman's research (2017) investigated the link between the need to belong and self-presentation among adolescents on Instagram. Their study found a significant positive correlation between Instagram self-presentation and the need to belong. Li (2022) delved into the relationship between social media use and the need to belong in young adults, establishing a positive association between the two. Individuals with a higher need to belong tended to use social media more frequently, putting them at a higher risk of potential exclusion. Given that previous studies (Li, 2022; Sarita & Suleeman, 2017) were conducted in non-Western countries, namely Indonesia and China, our study aims to explore potential cultural differences in the perception and sensitivity to social media ostracism. Büttner and Rudert's original study (2022) examined whether not being tagged and being excluded in real life evoked similar negative responses, finding a strong threat to fundamental needs when not being tagged in 2

a photo. While the aforementioned studies focused on different goals and investigated various correlations, a common thread emerged: participants with a higher need to belong tended to use social media more frequently and engage in higher self-presentation on Instagram. Our study extends this exploration to both Instagram and Facebook, anticipating potential differences in results due to distinct user demographics and platform features. Beyond cultural differences, demographic variances may influence social media use. Hruska and Maresova's study (2020) analyzed social media activity among over 2000 U.S. respondents with high household income and education levels. The results indicated that this demographic group exhibited the highest social media usage. Moreover, social media use tended to decrease with age, aligning with the assumption that young people, who have a higher need to belong, engage more with social media. In summary, the existing literature points to the nuanced dynamics of social media ostracism, motivating our study to explore potential cultural and demographic influences on this phenomenon across Instagram and Facebook platforms. 4. Research and KI-use Documentation For the literature review, a preliminary search was conducted using Elicit.org and Consensus.app, two AI-powered search engines designed to help researchers identify relevant papers and findings in the academic literature. This is achieved by formulating research questions. To begin our exploration of the topic, we asked the AI, "Does social media affect feelings of ostracism?" Although this method yielded several interesting papers, we also conducted additional searches using Google Scholar and PubMed databases. It is to be mentioned that we used ChatGPT throughout the process of our research to translate the original questionnaire into English and also as an aid for structuring texts. Between May and September 2023, we searched for relevant articles related to our research topic. Applying strategies such as 'building blocks' and 'berry picking', we used keywords such as 'social ostracism', 'social media exclusion', 'ostracism on Facebook', 'cultural differences in social media exclusion', 'cultural differences in 3

social media use', 'need to belong and social media', and 'temporal need-threat model' to conduct our research. In PubMed, our search string was "social ostracism or exclusion and social media or online or Instagram or Facebook," which yielded 333,079 results. To narrow down the selection, we applied filters for publications since 2017 and freely available full texts, resulting in 124,475 hits. After sorting the results by relevance, we focused on the first 30 outcomes. A similar approach was employed for the Google Scholar database, utilizing the same search string, which produced 5,450 results. Restricting the publication timeframe to "since 2017" left us with 4,140 results. After sorting for relevance, we examined the first 30 entries. Of the 60 relevant results in total, 34 were excluded due to the unavailability of full texts, and an additional 21 were thematically incompatible. Consequently, we identified and analyzed 5 papers for inclusion in this handout. 4

PRISMA flow chart based on Page et al. (2021) 5. Methodical approach The deliberation of our group was to replicate study 1 of the original study from M. Büttner and C. Rudert (2022) with a more heterogenous sample compared to our fellow students. To achieve this, we decided to recruit participants from diverse cultural backgrounds by conducting our study in different countries. Specifically, we planned to involve students from Maastricht University and the University of Basel, both renowned for their diverse and international student populations. 5

Our second deliberation was to add another social media platform to our research field that was not to be found in the original study from M. Büttner and C. Rudert, namely Facebook. Here, the idea was to find the differences and compare the Instagram and Facebook group with each other. We hypothesized to find differences between the Instagram and Facebook group for different reasons. Firstly, from personal experience, we assumed those participants indicating Facebook as their preferred platform to be older on average than those favoring Instagram. Such differences in age may show in scores of the ‘need to belong publicly’. Moreover, Facebook as a platform is older than Instagram and employs different usage strategies, targeting a different population than Instagram (Limniou et al., 2022). 6. Pre-Registration OSF-Link: https://osf.io/pkbj2 7. Results To investigate the association between the four specified exclusion scenarios (not tagged in a story, not tagged in a post, not visible in content, and cropped out of a photo) and subjective feelings (rated on a scale from 1 = good to 9 = bad), we conducted a repeated measures ANOVA. Due to the limited number of participants experiencing the 'cropped out' situation (N = 6), it was excluded from the analysis. The ANOVA results indicated a statistically significant difference across exclusion scenarios (p = 0.02, with Greenhouse-Geisser Correction), with no significant interaction observed between exclusion scenarios and the platform used (p = 0.63, with Greenhouse-Geisser correction). Post hoc tests with Bonferroni correction were subsequently conducted, revealing a significant difference only between the 'exclusion post' and 'exclusion not visible' scenarios (p = 0.01). No significant differences were found among the other exclusion situations. Additionally, a significant between-subjects effect was identified for the variable 'preferred platform' (p = 0.17, η² = 0.05). Consequently, independent t-tests were performed separately for each exclusion scenario to explore effects on both Facebook and Instagram. 6
